questions.prompt: A BJT in the active (forward-active) region has:
questions.explanation: Active region (normal amplifier operation): E-B forward biased (~0.7 V), B-C reverse biased.

questions.prompt: The DC current gain β of a BJT is defined as:
questions.explanation: β (h_FE) = I_C / I_B. Typical values: 50–500. Also α = I_C / I_E = β/(β+1).

questions.prompt: The small-signal transconductance g_m of a BJT is:
questions.explanation: g_m = I_C / V_T where V_T = kT/q ≈ 26 mV. At I_C = 1 mA: g_m = 1 mA / 26 mV ≈ 38.5 mA/V.

questions.prompt: The small-signal input resistance r_π of a BJT is:
questions.explanation: r_π = β/g_m = β V_T / I_C. Both are equivalent. Also written as h_ie in h-parameters.

questions.prompt: The common-emitter (CE) amplifier configuration has:
choices: A. High voltage gain, high current gain, 180° phase inversion
B. Unity voltage gain, high current gain
C. High voltage gain, no phase inversion
D. Low input impedance, high voltage gain
questions.explanation: CE: A_v = −g_m R_C (inverting), high A_i = β, moderate R_in = r_π, moderate R_out.

questions.prompt: The common-collector (emitter follower) configuration has:
choices: A. Voltage gain ≈ 1, high current gain, no phase inversion
B. Large voltage gain and 180° phase inversion
questions.explanation: Emitter follower (CC): A_v ≈ 1 (slightly less), high input impedance, low output impedance—ideal buffer.

questions.prompt: The common-base (CB) configuration has:
choices: A. High voltage gain, current gain < 1 (α), no phase inversion
questions.explanation: CB: A_i = α ≈ 1 (but < 1), high voltage gain, low input impedance, no phase inversion. Used at high frequencies.

questions.prompt: For a CE amplifier with R_C = 5 kΩ and I_C_Q = 1 mA, the small-signal voltage gain |A_v| = g_m × R_C is:
DC biasing for a BJT amplifier must set the Q-point in the ______ for linear amplification.
questions.explanation: The Q-point (quiescent operating point) must be in the active region for linear (undistorted) amplification.

questions.prompt: A voltage-divider bias circuit uses two resistors R1 and R2 to set V_B. The Thevenin base voltage is:
questions.explanation: Voltage divider: V_B = V_TH = V_CC × R2/(R1+R2) (R2 connected from base to ground).

questions.prompt: BJT saturation (for a switch) requires:
questions.explanation: Saturation: both junctions forward-biased; I_B must exceed I_C(sat)/β = V_CC/(β R_C) to force the transistor fully ON.

questions.prompt: The Early effect in a BJT refers to:
choices: A. Increased I_C with increasing V_CE at constant V_BE
B. Decreased β at high temperatures
questions.explanation: Early effect (base width modulation): I_C increases slightly with V_CE because the depletion region narrows the base. Modeled by Early voltage V_A.

questions.prompt: Adding an emitter bypass capacitor C_E in a CE amplifier:
choices: B. Increases the AC voltage gain by removing R_E from the gain formula
questions.explanation: C_E bypasses R_E at AC: A_v increases from −g_m(R_C‖R_E) to −g_m R_C. DC bias stability maintained by R_E.

questions.prompt: The output resistance r_o of a BJT (due to Early effect) is approximately:
questions.explanation: r_o = V_A / I_C where V_A is the Early voltage (typically 50–200 V). High r_o → more ideal current source behavior.

questions.prompt: The relationship between α and β of a BJT is:
Which BJT bias configurations provide good Q-point stability against β variation? (Select ALL that apply.)
choices: A. Voltage-divider bias with emitter resistor R_E
B. Fixed bias (single base resistor only)
questions.explanation: Fixed bias depends heavily on β. VD bias, collector-feedback, and emitter-feedback all provide negative feedback to stabilize the Q-point.

questions.prompt: In the hybrid-π model of a BJT, the current source is:
questions.explanation: Hybrid-π: g_m v_π = β i_b = (v_π/r_π) × β = g_m v_π. All are equivalent for AC small-signal analysis.

questions.prompt: The Miller effect in a CE amplifier causes the effective input capacitance to be:
questions.explanation: Miller effect: C_in = C_μ(1 + g_m R_C) = C_bc(1 + |A_v|). This greatly limits high-frequency response.

questions.prompt: A BJT biased with I_C_Q = 2 mA. The transconductance g_m in mA/V is approximately:
The unity-gain bandwidth f_T of a BJT is defined as the frequency at which |β(jf)| = 1. It equals approximately:
questions.explanation: f_T = g_m/(2π(C_π + C_μ)) = β f_β where f_β is the −3 dB frequency of β. Both forms are correct.

questions.prompt: A pnp transistor in active mode has conventional current flowing:
choices: A. Into collector and base, out of emitter
B. Out of collector, into emitter; emitter current is largest
D. Only from emitter to base
questions.explanation: pnp: conventional current flows from emitter (P) to collector (N). I_E = I_C + I_B; I_E > I_C > I_B.

questions.prompt: Coupling capacitors in multi-stage BJT amplifiers serve to:
choices: A. Block AC signals between stages
B. Pass AC signals while blocking DC bias levels from one stage to another
questions.explanation: Coupling capacitors: low impedance at signal frequencies → pass AC; block DC → each stage sets its own Q-point independently.

questions.prompt: The thermal stability factor S = ΔI_C / ΔI_CO for a BJT bias circuit: smaller S means:
choices: A. More Q-point drift with temperature
B. More stable Q-point against temperature changes
questions.explanation: S = ΔI_C/ΔI_CO. Smaller S → more stable. S = 1 is ideal. Voltage-divider bias achieves low S.

questions.prompt: A BJT circuit: V_CC = 12 V, R_B = 470 kΩ, R_C = 4.7 kΩ, β = 100. Fixed-bias I_C_Q ≈ ? (V_BE = 0.7 V)
When a BJT is used as a digital switch, it operates between:
questions.explanation: Digital switch: OFF = cut-off (I_B = 0, V_CE ≈ V_CC); ON = saturation (V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V).
